speaker: interviewer
text: Today's date is June 15, 1995. The survivor we're interviewing is Max Rosenbloom-- R-O-S-E-N-B-L-O-O-M-- who at birth was Rosenblum, spelled R-O-S-E-N-B-L-U-M. The interviewer today is Sandra Giddens-- G-I-D-D-E-N-S. The city is Toronto, country Canada. And we're conducting the interview in English. And these I don't have to spell, right? Today's date is June 15, 1995. The survivor we're interviewing today is Max Rosenbloom. My name is Sandra Giddens, I'm the interviewer. We're in the city of Toronto, the country of Canada. Please tell us your name today.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Max Rosenbloom.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how do you spell your last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: R-O-S-E-N-B-L-double-O-M.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you were born, what was your name, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name was Matys, M-A-T-Y-S.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rosenblum.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you spell it at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: R-O-S-E-N-B-L-- double-- U-M.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please tell us your date of birth.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born on December 12, 1925.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your age today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Today, 68-- 69 and 1/2, exactly-- 69 and 1/2.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us your place of birth.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Bogoria.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you please spell Bogoria for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: B-O-R-- B-O-R-G-O-R-I-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where is Bogoria?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you take us back to Poland as a young child and tell us what it was like growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was born, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in Poland, in Bogoria, I can recall as a child, my father and mother were separated. And then I went-- my mother took me to a farm, which my mother's sisters and her mother was on this farm. They were farm [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us what the name of your mother and the mother's sister and your grandmother-- what their names were?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name was Reisl.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Feinkuchen. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] If you want to-- to spell is a little bit-- I have to write it down. And then my grandmother's name was also Sura-- a Jewish name, Sura. And then I had aunts. One was Marmu, and one was Rifki-- two Ns.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was the farm?

speaker: interviewee
text: The farm was also in Poland, not far from Bogoria-- about 20, 25 kilometers. This was the farm. And then I was grown up in that farm. And I can recall when I was about three, four years old, you know, I was still growing up there. And when I got to the age of seven, school age, I moved-- they took me-- my father took me back from that farm, and they took me to the city of Staszow.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Staszow, I started school as a child. And I was going to heder there. And this was in 19-- I don't, you know. Who remembers the dates exactly? I don't. Tell us about your schools.

speaker: interviewee
text: The schooling-- you know, this was-- when I started school, I was very good in Polish, because I was growing up on a farm between Poles. And then the Jewish kids, which were going to that school, they didn't know a word of Polish. I was kind of a [NON-ENGLISH]-- [NON-ENGLISH] to those kids. When the teacher wanted to know when the kids said something, I have to be there, and I have to [NON-ENGLISH] for them, because I was very good in Polish. I was talking to them, and they didn't know till later years. You know, a kid when he started at seven years old, he learns fast the language.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they-- they afterwards, for about five, three months, four months, five months later, they were talking, and they didn't need me. But I was growing up in the school from grade 1 up to grade 13. And tell us about--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland, there was no grade 13. It was grade seven. From grade one up to grade seven. This was seven years. And then the war broke out-- 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about heder. What was heder like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Heder was just like Jewish kids after school. You know, four hours, we went to school in the morning, 8 o'clock started school, the Polish school. And we went there till around 1 o'clock. Then I came home. I grabbed a piece of bread-- that's about it-- and I went to heder. And there was a teacher, you know, teached you just Jewish and all the things, you know, like [NON-ENGLISH] and all those things-- you know, reading Hebrew, writing Hebrew, writing Jewish. This was the heder years.

speaker: interviewer
text: What languages were spoken in the home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Strictly Yiddish, Jewish, nothing else. Between our brothers and sisters and father, all the family, nothing else was spoken but Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us how many brothers and sisters you had?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had brothers. I had-- But I have to tell you that-- that the brothers what I had with sisters previously, there was just brothers from the same father, not from the same mother. Because when my father got married, he got seven kids, six kids. And then his wife passed away, and he remarried my mother. And I only had one sister, which she's not alive, with the same father and the same mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were like yours, mine, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], all kind of things, separated. So I had about four brothers, five brothers, and one sister. And this is from the same father, not from the same mother. Can you tell us the names of all of them from the same father?

speaker: interviewee
text: The oldest brother was Moishe, and then was Motl, then was Haskel, and then was Rivchu, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and [? Kalme. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you place in that order?

speaker: interviewee
text: That order exactly-- the way they were-- Moishe was the first, oldest. And then was Morl, which is still alive. He's in Toronto. And then was Haskel, which I was with him in the concentration camp, and they shot him and then Rivchu. This is what you have in the picture here. And then was [? Kalme ?], Karl, they're calling him here. He's still alive. He's here. We were together in camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you like growing up? What kind of child were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: What I was like-- I was very wild. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was fighting with everybody in school, with all the boys, with the Jewish boys, with Christian boys, you know. We were always fighting.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe an incident.

speaker: interviewee
text: Incidents, I can describe you one, that at one time I had a fight with a Gentile boy. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he was in the same class with me. This was probably the class-- in the fourth class, which was the fourth year. And we went out from the-- from the-- in-- in-- in-- outside, and he started to start up with me, you know, to fight. So I nearly killed him. I nearly killed him. I-- you know, I took him apart. And then he ran home. He was bleeding all over. And the teachers came in and everything, and they started to straighten out things-- what happened and everything. He ran home. He organized the whole city, practically-- the whole city-- boys, young boys, Christian boys. And they came, in while I was going out. They wanted to catch me. They didn't bother anybody else. They wanted to catch me. And you know, probably they would have killed me that time. But I have seen-- I was the last one out from this school, and I've seen what's going on. And the boys started to yell, run away, because they're going to kill you. So I ran back to the school. And our school was situated-- like a cemetery was behind the school, a Jewish cemetery an old-- called Old Jewish Cemetery. And there was a wall all around that cemetery, a big wall. And you know, you have monuments there and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I ran back to the school. I opened up a window, and I jumped out through the window to the cemetery at night. At night, and I was there. That time, I was 10 years old, maybe.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And through the cemetery, I ran to the end of the cemetery. And I jumped over the fence-- that big fence. And I came home through the back door. And I came into the house, and I passed out, because as a young boy, you know, to go in a cemetery at night, this was-- this was not heard of in the small towns, because everybody was afraid of a cemetery.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I came home, and I just passed out. And I didn't know what happened. So I started-- I came through, and I started to tell them. That's what happened. The next day, I went to school, and nobody said a word. Nobody-- they didn't know. They thought that it was a miracle, that I disappeared. How did I get out from the school not noticing? And they checked every guy which went by. That's not him. That's not him. That's not him. You know, they checked everybody. And they didn't know what happened till late, late, I told them what happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they couldn't believe it, that I-- at night, to go in a cemetery, this was lunacy, you know, as a boy. At home, was, you know, like, terrible. So this what happened. This was the incident. But I was very wild, and I was practically fighting with everybody as a young boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: What subjects where you good at in school?

speaker: interviewee
text: What subject? Oh, mostly was like regular subjects, history, geography, arithmetic, all kind of subject, the same what's here, you know, mathematics and everything, everything. As a matter of fact, we had in the school, we had a teacher, a Jewish guy, learned, which we had once a week with classes of Jewish history.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember teachers' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. But we had a teacher, Jewish teacher, which one, the Jewish boys with the girls, you know, once a week-- the girls had separate. We didn't go with girls with boys together. It was girls' classes and boys' classes. So we had those once a week. We had Jewish history. Torah-- he teaches us like in the heder, something like that. This was once a week. And the rest was all Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you come from a religious family background?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not strictly orthodox, but fairly religious. My father was a very religious man. Without-- He couldn't live without the shul or not to pray every day, three times a day, or anything like it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us your father's name and a little bit more about his personality?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Avram Abe. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] His personality, as far as I know-- I just learned from him when I came back to the city of Staszow from that farm, because otherwise I didn't see him. I never heard of him, because when my mother separated from him when I was very young, maybe two or three years old. That's about it. But in Staszow, he wasn't working. He was a very sick man. He had asthma, and he couldn't work. But before that, before he got married the second time, he was a merchant. And he had a store, [? lettuce ?] store, what they say-- a store in that small town, Bogoria. He had a store, and that's what the family made a living from. And he was quite good in it, and you know, they made a living. But when I came back, he wasn't working. Our brothers were working, like Haskel, my sister, and my brother, which is here, Karl, they were working. We were working at home. We were shoemakers, like making the tops of shoes. [NON-ENGLISH], that's what they called that at home.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were working, contracting for a big storekeeper, which he had shoes. He was selling shoes. So they were working. My father was helping as much as he could, and that's what we made a living from. What was he like as a father? Was he lenient? Was he strict?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as a father I can tell you that-- a father, you know-- that he never bothered too much with me, you know, because he was himself not-- whatever I did was OK-- not too direct or-- like here a father is, like, I notice in my own son-in-law, you know, his boy comes home from school, what did you do? How did you do it? And come on, I want to hear what you know, how you're learning, things like that. Not my father, no. it was just plain and simple. Whatever I did, it was OK. Whatever I-- as long as I didn't bother him too much.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your mother? Describe her personality.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was in that farm, you know, my mother was by herself, and she had to support me. She was working very hard, very hard.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the farm?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not on the farm. She was-- beside what she was working on the farm, she was like a peddler. She was going all over the-- between the farmers she was gathering, like, bottles and selling something, and things like that. From that, she made a living to support me. And I was with her till seven years old. And then, somehow, afterwards, when I came back to Staszow from that farm, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] somehow they got together back, my father and mother. Whether through me or not, they got together back. But there was the only thing that's supporting what was from my brothers and sisters, which they were working. And this was the support, what we got, all of them. Otherwise, there was nothing was major to come in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother make any traditional suppers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Oh, yeah. She was together with us, sure. We were all together. She was the only one to cook for everybody, you know, to clean, to cook. The whole family was living in one room-- one room. It was two beds, and it was there we were working in that same room at daytime. At nighttime, we pushed away on the side, and we took out some boards with chairs put together, and we were sleeping in it. with a-- our mattress was straw stuffed in a bag. This was the mattress.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're talking about good times, you know? Reminding myself about those things, don't worry. This stays with you-- not everything, exactly, but most of it. Can you describe an incident with the family gathering together in this one room?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. This was very nice, lovingly. You know, this was not like families here-- jealousy, with things like that. It was never-- because nobody never had nothing, nobody-- everybody was eating when we came, when we brought the bread. So everybody was equally divided the bread. And whatever was there to eat, there was no jealousy between one another, you know. There was lovingly, you know that we were-- everybody was for everybody. And whenever they made-- $1 or $2, or whatever it is-- they would share. And this was the life, what went down in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about, are you going to synagogue? Did you ever go with your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Oh, every-- every Saturday. Saturday, Friday night, all the holidays, and everything-- there was not a day that I didn't go. I didn't have no choice, whether I liked it or not. I have to go. Not the older brothers-- they didn't-- they didn't participate in those things, because they were already like the bread winners, so he couldn't say to them, you have to go, you have to do. So they went on their own, and they did whatever they wanted.

speaker: interviewer
text: What literature was brought into the household?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish. Jewish papers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the names of any of the newspapers? [NON-ENGLISH] There was [NON-ENGLISH] too, you know. There was-- It wasn't printed in Staszow. It was printed in the biggest cities, and it was sent there. Once a week, there came a paper. And everybody was buying it, and everybody was reading it. This was-- and most of the time, they were reading Jewish books, not Polish. Polish, not so much, but Jewish books. Can you describe your street, what it was like?

speaker: interviewee
text: The street? The street was like-- the whole street was called [NON-ENGLISH], you know, like the synagogue was there. From my house to go to the synagogue was about five minutes. This was the only beautiful synagogue in the city, not just in the city of Staszow but in the whole Poland. One of the nicest synagogues there is, and near the synagogue was a [NON-ENGLISH]. You know, like another [NON-ENGLISH], like a gathering. And we were davening in that-- not in that shul, because this shul was a Conservative shul. You know, when something came out, you know, to celebrate, like, the first of May or the third of May-- Poland, we were celebrating 3rd of May. And so they had the celebration at shul with the hazzan and the Polish dignitaries from city. They came there, and there was [NON-ENGLISH] for the President, you know, Joseph Stalin-- not Joseph Stalin, Jozef Pilsudski This was very good for the Jews in Poland. So there was a whole celebration, the third of May. And they all came to that shul, and this was a beautiful shul. [INAUDIBLE] was going, like, practically every day. When I was 13 years-- I still remember-- when I was bar mitzvahed, it was 1939. And the Germans came in. They were already in Poland. 1939, my father took me into that [NON-ENGLISH], and they gave me aliyah, and this was my bar mitzvah. And they told me, from now on, you're on your own. You have to put tefillin and tallis every morning. You have to be a good Jew. This was my bar mitzvah. This is what I remember-- 1939. And then my mother died the same-- the same time. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And she was buried in Staszow in that cemetery. This was also 1939, and I still remember I was saying Kaddish practically the whole year, till 1940. 1940, they took me away from Staszow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we get to that, was your father or anybody in the family politically oriented?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who had in mind even to think about politics? The only struggle which was there-- to make a living, to make barely to go by every day. And this was the main struggle at home.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about anti-Semitism, besides at school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like I just described you. You see, like, we had a gymnasium in-- in Staszow-- very nice, higher education. like, when you finished school. You were 13 years old when you finished school-- seven grades. And when you had the means, you want to go for higher education. This was called gymnasium. You know, and some boys, Jewish boys, which they were very well-to-do-- you know, merchants and stores, you know, very well-to-do-- they wanted to go to that school. So they have to pay a lot of money to go into that school, but they suffered very bad. They are harassed. The harassment was very great anti-Semitism. In particular, 1938, 1939, before the Germans came to Poland. This was barely, because the fights were going on there day after day, week after week.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the police? Did they ever get involved?

speaker: interviewee
text: They got involved, so they always blamed the Jewish-- the Jewish kids. They didn't blame them, that they started the whole thing. But they blamed the Jewish kids. This was terrible, terrible. We had-- I had here one from Staszow, [INAUDIBLE]. He died. He was, like, called-- the whole city of Staszow, there were 7,000 Jews in Staszow. The whole city of Staszow-- the Poles, the Gentile people-- they were afraid for him. They called him my bandit. A bandit, they called him. If they have seen him, they run away. He was fighting with every Pollock which was there in the city. And they couldn't get out alive in his hands. He died here in Toronto. He came. He lived through the war, concentration camps, and he died. But they were afraid. Everybody was afraid for him in Staszow. And he was fighting with everybody. But the anti-Semitism was still growing. As they come closer with the Germans, it became very bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about you as a young boy? Did you have any dreams before the war broke out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure, I had dreams. Which child didn't have a dream? I should have enough to eat and some clothes on my back-- this was my dream all the time. Something good to eat, because there was never something good-- there was bread, just bread. And when I came home from school, I remember times, which-- when I had a piece of bread and a piece of onions-- raw onions with bread-- it was good. And I took a piece with a piece of-- my mother gave me a piece of onion. Take a piece of onion with the piece of bread, stale bread. She was hiding from me because I was young growing up.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was it. I grabbed this, and I went out to play, you know, with boys and girls, things like that. That's what this was. This was the life-- nothing, nothing, nothing good. If you want to talk-- I don't know about the other ones, but in the small towns, in the small cities, there was no picnic, believe me, growing up as a child, particularly, except if the families were wealthy. But who was wealthy there? Nobody. Nobody had nothing. Some kids, like across the street where I was living, there was a boy from the mine. Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Szmulek Jaskulka [INAUDIBLE]-- so this was his name. So he was my best boy friend. You know, I was living this side of the street, and he was living across the street. They were already in a better position than we were. In their house, you-- when you came in, you seen, they had a good dinner, you know-- meat at the dinner and and-- bread, butter, all kind of milks, and you know, good things when they came in summer. There was no fridges then. Fridge was a-- not a thing in the vocabulary yet in Poland at this time. But they had all those good things, you know, to eat, so I was always envy-- to see them, their living. You know, we used to go out together-- very good friends. So this was the-- the things which you lived through in life as a young child-- nothing good, nothing good, but memories.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to stop here, and next tape we'll go on into the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
